title: Eastern European Card to West African Shipping Drop Pattern
narrative: Cardholder presents legitimate European-issued card (BIN 4462xx, 5134xx) with Polish or Romanian billing address, but ships high-value electronics ($800-$2,200 per transaction) to Nigeria, Ghana, or Kenya. Orders placed between 02:00-06:00 UTC when European cardholders typically sleep. Email domains are free providers (gmail.com, yahoo.com) rather than regional ISPs. The card shows clean 90-day history with domestic transactions averaging $45-$120, then suddenly 3-6 international shipments within 72 hours. Ship-to name rarely matches cardholder name on file. Fraudster exploits stolen card data before legitimate holder notices, using freight forwarders or accomplices at destination. Product category shifts from groceries/fuel (MCC 5411, 5541) to consumer electronics (MCC 5732, 5946). Distance between billing addr1 and shipping R_addr1 exceeds 4,500 km (dist1 feature). Pattern terminates when card issuer blocks international transactions or cardholder disputes charges.
indicators: dist1 > 4500 km between billing and shipping coordinates
card1_txn_count_24hr shows 3-6 transactions versus 90-day baseline of 8-12 monthly
txn_hour between 2-6 (is_night=1) for European BIN with African R_addr1 country code
P_emaildomain and R_emaildomain both free providers (gmail, yahoo, outlook) not matching card BIN country
card1_amt_zscore > 3.5 (current transaction amount far exceeds historical mean)
MCC code shift from 5411/5541 (groceries/fuel) to 5732/5946 (electronics) within same card1 session
summary: A European payment card with months of small domestic purchases suddenly makes multiple high-value electronics orders shipping to West Africa during European night hours, using free email providers and mismatched recipient names.
reasoning: Fraudsters exploit stolen card details before the legitimate cardholder wakes up and notices, rushing expensive goods to overseas accomplices or freight forwarders who can liquidate electronics quickly in destination markets.
indicator_explanations: Card billing address is thousands of kilometers from shipping destination
High-value electronics purchase shipped internationally to unfamiliar region
Card suddenly active multiple times today versus typical monthly usage
European card used overnight to ship goods to African address
Both buyer and recipient use free email providers mismatched to card country
Purchase amount dramatically exceeds cardholder's normal spending pattern
Card switches from everyday purchases to expensive electronics within hours
